title: 第 22 節
en: p 3 214 SUTRA LITERATURE IN GENERAL.

tary treatise on astronomy. This supplementary treatise is one of the Parisishtas, and in the same class of writings we meet with other tracts on astronomical subjects, such as the Gobhiliya Navagrahasanti-parisishta 1 belonging to the Sama-veda, and several more belonging to the Atharva-veda.2 If now we take a comprehensive view of that class of literature which we have just examined, we find some characteristic features throughout. All these works were written with a practical object, quite a new phase in the literature of such a nation as the ancient Hindus. The only authority which the Sutrakaras, the authors of the Sutras, claimed for their works was the authority of that ancient, and, as it was then already considered, revealed literature on which their works were founded. These men claimed no inspiration for themselves. They had made a scientific study of the literature handed down to them by former generations, and they wished to make that study easier to their contemporaries and to future generations. The style which they adopted for that purpose was business-like in the extreme. It was the curt and dry style of the Sutras, a style peculiar to India, which can only be compared with the elaborate tables of contents, or the marginal notes, of some of our own early writers. It has its first beginnings in the Brahmanas, where some subjects, particularly those which had given rise to early controversy, are stated with all the conciseness and neatness of the Sutra style. But whereas the authors 1 MS. Chambers, 404.
2 Nakshatrakalpa, Grahayuddha, Rahuchara, Ketuchara, Rituketulakshana, Nakshatragrahotpatalakshana. "Weber, Ind. Stud, i. 87. 100.

ANUKRAMANis. 215 of the Brahmanas screened their poverty behind a constant display of the most inane verbosity, the writers of the Sutras gloried in every word they could save without endangering the practical usefulness of their manuals. In some instances they adopted a poetical form, and they succeeded in combining the conciseness of their prose with the rhythm of their early metres, the mixed &lokas. Thus their position is marked by the very form of their works, as intermediate between the antique style of the Brahmanas, and the modern style of the metrical Sastras. Their works form a distinct and compact class of literature, and if we succeed in fixing the relative age of any one of these Sutrakaras or writers of Sutras, we shall have fixed the age of a period of literature which forms a transition between the Vedic and the classical literature of India.
THE ANUKRAMANis.

Several of the works mentioned before were ascribed to Saunaka and his two pupils, Katyayana and Asvalayana. But we have not yet mentioned a number of treatises, ascribed to the same authors, and belonging to the same sphere of literature as the Sutras, which, however, on account of their technical character, could not lay claim to the title of Vedanga, or " member of the Veda." They are known by the name of the Anukramanis, from anu, along, and Jcram, to step. They are systematic indices to various portions of the ancient Yedic literature.

The most perfect Anukramani is that of the Sanhita of the Rig-veda. It is ascribed to Katyayana, an author chiefly known by his works on the Yajurveda and Sama-veda. Its name is Sarvanukramani p 4 216 anukramanIs.

or Sarvanukrama, i. e. the index of all things.1 It gives the first words of each hymn, the number of verses, the name and family of the poets, the names of the deities, and the metres of every verse. Before the time of Katyayana, there had been separate indices for each of these subjects, and it was with reference to them that Katyayana called his own index the general or comprehensive index. Our authority for this is Shadgurusishya, the author of a commentary on the Index of Katyayana ; a man who like Devarajayajvan, the author of a commentary on the Nighantu, was not without a certain appreciation of the historical progress of Indian literature. He tells us in his Yedarthadipika, that before Katyayana, there existed one index of the poets, one of the metres, one of the deities, one of the Anuvakas, the old chapters of the Rig-veda, and one of the hymns ; 2 and that these indices were composed by Saunaka. Now we know the style of Saunaka, and as by a happy accident some of these former indices have been preserved, some complete, others in fragments, we are able to test Shadgurusishya's accuracy.

We remarked before, as a distinctive peculiarity of the style of Saunaka, as contrasted with that of Katyayana, that the Pratisakhja ascribed to the former is composed in mixed Slokas, whereas the Pratisakhya of Katyayana is written in prose or in Sutras. The same observation applies to the Anu- ANUKRAMANIS. 217 kramanis. Those ascribed to Saunaka are composed in mixed metres, as far as we can judge from quotations ; the Anukramani of his pupil Katyayana is in prose, and exhibits all the artificial appliances of a Sutra composition. There is one of Saunaka's Anukramanis, the Anuvaka-anukramani, which can be restored completely from MSS.1 ; and this work bears the most manifest traces of Saunaka's style, partly in the mixture, partly in a peculiar rudeness, of its metres. The other Anukramanis ascribed to Saunaka are lost to us, but they must have existed at the time of Shadgurusishya. He quotes not only from the Anuvaka-anukramani (Bhashya, viii. 1.), but also from the Deva-anukrama (Bhashya, viii. 4.), and he distinguishes this work from the Brihaddevata, another work attributed to Saunaka, of which there is one MS. in Europe at the Eoyal Library of Berlin.2 Sayana also, though later than Shadgurusishya, was still in possession of Saunaka's works, and he quotes particularly the Bri- 1 Several MSS. contain portions of the Anuvakanukramani ; and with the help of Shadgurusishya' s Commentary, contained in the introduction to his commentary on Katyayana's Sarvanukrama, (MS. Bodl. "Wilson, 379.), the text might be published in a critical edition.

2 Dr. Kuhn gives the following description of this MS. in Haupt's " Zeitschrift fur Deutsches Alterthum." The Brihaddevata (Chambers, 192.) composed in epical metre, is ascribed to 6aunaka, and contains an enumeration of the deities invoked in each hymn of the Rig-veda. It gives much mythological and other information as to the character of the gods of the Veda. The text of the MS. is so corrupt that we can scarcely think of restoring it without the help of other MSS." Another MS. has since been found in India, and a distinguished Sanskrit scholar is preparing an edition of it.
218 ANUKRAMANis.

haddevata, in several of his own commentaries. Saunaka's Arsha-anukramani is quoted by Sayana in his Commentary on the Rig-veda, i. 100. 1. If we add to these quotations a reference to Saunaka's Chhando'nukramani, which is found in Shadgurusishya's Vedarthadipika (MS. E.I.H. 1823, p. 7.' a.), we may consider the authenticity of these works sufficiently established ; and it is hardly unreasonable to suppose that the fifth Anukramani also, of which no quotations have as yet been met' with, the Suktanukramani, was in existence as late as Sayana' s time.1 This would give us for the Rig-veda five Anukramanis by Saunaka, one by Katyayana, and one by an unknown author. The Brihaddevata is a work of too large a compass to be called an Anukramani, and it is even doubtful whether we possess this work in the same form in which iSaunaka left it. To judge from Dr. Kuhn's extracts, the author of the Brihaddevata follows indeed the Sakala-sakha, but his text must have differed from that of our MSS. The author may have followed one of the subdivisions of the Sakalas, the Saisira-sakha, for instance, which we know was followed by Saunaka. The division of the Sanhita which is adopted in the Brihaddevata, is that of Mandalas, Anuvakas, and Suktas ; but the other division into Ashtakas is equally known, and even the Khilas are taken into account, whereas both i§aunaka and Katyayana exclude these later hymns dis- 1 Another Anukramani, containing the last verses of each Mandala, is quoted by Shadgurusishya (Anukr. Bh. viii. 1.).
Cf. Iiv. Mand. vii. 6. 15 ; Asht. v. 7. 9.

ANUKRAMANis. 219 tinctly from their indices. Dr. Kuhn concludes from a passage in Shadgurusishya's Commentary, to which we shall revert hereafter, that not Saunaka, but Asvalayana, was the author of our Brihaddevata. This conclusion, however, is not borne out by sufficient evidence, nor is the fact that Saunaka is quoted by name in the work itself a sufficient argument against (Saunaka's authorship. According to the line of argument adopted by Dr. Kuhn, it would be equally objectionable to ascribe the Brihaddevata to Asvalayana; for in one passage, according to Dr. Kuhn's own emendations, the name of Asvalayana also occurs in it. Other authorities which are quoted in this curious work are the Aitareyaka, the Kaushitakins, the Bhallavi-brahmana, the Nidana (nidanasanjnake granthe), Sakalas, Bashkalas, Madhuka, Svetaketu, Galava, Gargya, Rathitara, Rathantarin, iSakatayana, Sandilya, Romakayana Sthavira, Kathakya, Bhagurin, Sakapuni, Bharmyasva Mudgala, Aurnavabha, Kraushtukin, Matrin, and Yaska. The last is most frequently mentioned, and the whole book is dedicated to him. To judge from the style of the Brihaddevata, the work as we now possess it, though originally written by Saunaka, seems to have been recast by a later writer.

The following figures, taken from Saunaka's Anukramanis, will serve to give an idea of the minuteness with which the Yeda was studied at his time. According to iSaunaka, the Sakala-sakha of the Rigveda-sanhita consists of 10 Mandalas, or 64 Adhyayas.

msisting of 1 ii ^ n 7 64 Adhyayas = 2,006 = 10,417 Here we have to observe a difference between the number of verses, as deduced from the Vargas, and the number stated by Saunaka. The latter gives the 1 Trim satani shatkanam chatvarinsat shat cha vargah.
ANUKRAMANfS. 221 sum total of verses=10,580|, but, immediately afterwards, the sum total of half verses=21,232i= 10,616 verses.

How this difference arose it is difficult to say ; but it should be observed that, if we divide the sum total of half verses, 21,232, by 2, we get 10,616 verses, and this number comes very near to 10,622, which the Charanavyuha gives as the sum total of the verses of the Rig-veda. According to the Charanavyuha (MS. Ch. 785.) the 64 Adhyayas of the Rigveda have : —
Yargas consisting of rses Vargas.

Verses, The number of padas or words in the Rig-vedasanhita is stated as 153,826, which gives an average of between 14 to 15 words to each verse. Another computation brings the number of the charcha-padas (i.e. words which are used in the Kramapatha, omitting the repeated passages or galitas) to 110,704, and the number of syllables to 432,000.
In another Anukramani, Saunaka gives a list of verses, arranged according to the metres in which they are written ; and at the end he states the sum ANUKRAMANIS.
zh: 頁3 214 經文文獻概論

這是一篇關於天文學的補充論述。這部補充論述是《附篇》（Parisishtas）之一，在同一類著作中，我們還遇到其他天文學主題的論文，例如屬於《沙摩吠陀》（Sama-veda）的《高婆利耶九星和平附篇》（Gobhiliya Navagrahasanti-parisishta），以及屬於《阿闥婆吠陀》（Atharva-veda）的若干篇章。如果我們現在全面審視我們剛才考察過的這一類文獻，就會發現其中始終存在若干特徵。所有這些著作都是為實踐目的而著述，這對於古代印度人這樣的民族的文獻而言，是一個全新的階段。《經文作者》（Sutrakaras）、《經文》（Sutras）的著者為其作品所聲稱的唯一權威，是他們所基於的那種古老且當時已被視為啟示文獻的文學。這些人並未為自己聲稱有啟示。他們對前代傳承下來的文獻進行了科學研究，他們希望使這種研究對其同代人及後代更加容易。他們為此目的所採用的文風極其務實。這是《經文》特有的簡潔而枯燥的風格，一種印度特有的文體，只能與我們早期著作者的詳盡目錄表或邊註相比較。它在《梵書》（Brahmanas）中有其最初的萌芽，其中某些主題，特別是那些引起過早期爭議的主題，都以《經文》風格的所有簡潔和整齊性而表述。但是，而作者
2 Nakshatrakalpa, Grahayuddha, Rahuchara, Ketuchara, Rituketulakshana, Nakshatragrahotpatalakshana. "Weber, Ind. Stud, i. 87. 100.

婆羅門書（Brahmanas）的作者以最庸俗的冗長措辭源源不絕的展示來掩飾其貧乏，而經書（Sutras）的作者則以每一個他們能夠節省而不危害其手冊實用性的詞彙自豪。在某些情況下，他們採用了詩歌形式，並成功地將其散文的簡潔性與他們早期韻律相結合，即混合的頌歌（śloka）。因此他們的著作形式本身就標誌了他們的地位，介乎婆羅門書的古老風格與韻文《薩斯特拉》（Sastras）的現代風格之間。他們的著作構成了一個獨特而緊密的文學類別，若我們能夠確定任何一位經書作者（Sutrakaras）的相對年代，我們就將確定了一個文學時期的年代，該時期形成了吠陀與古典印度文學之間的過渡。

阿奴克拉瑪尼經（The Anukkramanis） --- **阿奴克拉瑪尼經** 阿奴克拉瑪尼經（Anukkramanis）是梨俱吠陀的目錄與索引，其名稱源自梵文 *anukramana*，意為「按順序排列」或「編目」。這些索引文獻對於理解吠陀經文的組織與傳承至關重要。 它們按照讚美詩的數量、神祇、詩人與韻律進行分類，為古代印度學者與祭司提供了系統性的導覽工具。阿奴克拉瑪尼經的存在證明了吠陀傳統中對於文本組織與精確記憶的高度重視。 通過這些目錄，我們得以窺見吠陀經典在口頭傳誦與書面記錄之間如何被整理與保存。阿奴克拉瑪尼經因此成為比較神話學與語言科學研究中不可或缺的輔助文獻。

前面提及的幾部著作被歸於紹那迦（Saunaka）及其兩位弟子迦梯耶那（Katyayana）和阿詩婆拉耶那（Asvalayana）名下。但我們尚未談及許多論著，同樣歸於這些作者名下，並屬於與經文（Sutra）相同的文獻範疇。然而，這些論著由於其專業技術性質，無法聲稱具有吠陀支（Vedanga）或「吠陀之肢」（"member of the Veda"）的名義。它們以**阿奴克拉瑪尼斯**（Anukramanis）之名為人所知，此詞源自 anu 一詞（沿着）及 kram 一詞（踏步）。它們是古代吠陀文獻各部分的系統索引。
最完美的《經文索引》(Anukramani) 是《梨俱吠陀集成本》(Sanhita of the Rig-veda) 的那部。它被歸於迦多衍那 (Katyayana)，此作者主要以其關於《夜柔吠陀》(Yajurveda) 和《娑摩吠陀》(Sama-veda) 的著作而聞名。其名稱為《全索引》(Sarvanukramani)，第 4 頁 216 條 anukramanIs。

或稱為《薩瓦努克拉瑪》（Sarvanukrama），即「萬物索引」。它記載每首讚歌的首句、詩句數目、詩人的名號與家族、神祇的名稱，以及每一詩句的韻律。在迦堤耶延（Katyayana）之前，各科目已有各自獨立的索引，迦堤耶延正是參照這些索引而將自己的索引稱為「通總索引」或「綜合索引」。我們的根據來自沙德古盧西希亞（Shadgurusishya），他是迦堤耶延《索引》注疏的作者；此人如同提婆羅闍耶吒婆（Devarajayajvan）——《詞彙編》（Nighantu）的注疏作者——一樣，對印度文獻的歷史進展具有相當的認識。他在其《韋達義燈》（Yedarthadipika）中告訴我們，在迦堤耶延之前，已存在詩人索引一種、韻律索引一種、神祇索引一種、阿努瓦卡索引一種（阿努瓦卡為梨俱吠陀的古老章節），以及讚歌索引一種；²而這些索引皆由紹納卡（Saunaka）所編纂。我們知曉紹納卡的文風，且由於幸運之故，這些早期索引中的某些得以保存——有的完整，有的為殘片——我們得以驗證沙德古盧西希亞的準確性。

我們之前已經注意到，作為沙烏那卡（Saunaka）文風與卡提亞耶那（Katyayana）文風相對照時的一個顯著特點，即歸屬於前者的《序言經》（Pratisakhja）是用混雜的頌體（mixed Slokas）而成，而卡提亞耶那的《序言經》則以散文或蘇特拉體（Sutras）寫成。同樣的觀察也適用於《索引經》（Anukramanis）。歸屬於沙烏那卡的那些作品是用混雜的韻律寫成的，就我們從引文所能判斷的範圍而言；他的弟子卡提亞耶那的《索引經》則以散文寫成，並展現了蘇特拉體著作的一切人為技巧。沙烏那卡有一部《索引經》名為《護誦索引經》（Anuvaka-anukramani），可以從手稿中完全復原；這部著作在沙烏那卡文風的特徵上留下了最明顯的蹤跡，部分體現在韻律的混雜上，部分體現在其韻律的一種特殊的粗糙性上。歸屬於沙烏那卡的其他《索引經》已經失傳於我們，但它們在沙德古如希什亞（Shadgurusishya）時代必然曾經存在。他不僅引用《護誦索引經》（《註釋》viii. 1.），也引用《提婆索引經》（Deva-anukrama）（《註釋》viii. 4.），並將這部著作與《大提婆經》（Brihaddevata）區分開來，後者是另一部歸屬於沙烏那卡的著作，在歐洲柏林皇家圖書館有一份手稿。薩耶那雖然晚於沙德古如希什亞，但仍然掌握著沙烏那卡的著作，他特別引用《大提婆經》

Dr. Kuhn 在 Haupt 的《德國古代研究期刊》（"Zeitschrift fur Deutsches Alterthum"）中對該手稿提供了以下描述。《廣天神書》（Brihaddevata，Chambers, 192.）以史詩韻文編撰而成，相傳出自 Saunaka 之手，其內容為梨俱吠陀各讚歌所祭祀提婆的列舉。該書就吠陀諸神的性質提供了大量神話學及其他資訊。該手稿的文本損壞甚重，以至於若無其他手稿之助，我們幾乎無法想像能將其復原。」自此之後，又在印度發現了另一份手稿，一位傑出的梵文學者正在為其編輯版本做準備。
218 阿奴克拉瑪尼（Anukramani）。

哈德瓦塔（haddevata），沙烏那卡在他自己的多部註釋中引述過。沙烏那卡的《阿爾沙目錄》（Arsha-anukramani）被沙亞那在其《梨俱吠陀註》（Commentary on the Rig-veda）第一卷第100頌1處所引述。如果我們將這些引文加上對沙烏那卡《韻文目錄》（Chhando'nukramani）的參考資料，後者見於沙德古魯希亞的《吠陀義光》（Vedarthadipika）（東方研究院手稿1823，第7頁a），那麼我們可認為這些著作的真實性已充分確立；並且假設第五部《目錄》（Anukramani）也曾存在至沙亞那的時代，並非全無理由，儘管迄今尚未發現對它的任何引述，該著作即為《讚歌目錄》（Suktanukramani）。這樣一來，我們對於梨俱吠陀便擁有沙烏那卡撰寫的五部《目錄》、卡提亞亞那撰寫的一部，以及作者不詳的一部。《大提婆書》（Brihaddevata）是篇幅過於龐大而難以稱為《目錄》的著作，且我們目前持有的本子是否與沙烏那卡留下的形式相同，甚至成疑。據庫恩博士的摘錄判斷，《大提婆書》的作者確實追循沙卡拉分派（Sakala-sakha），但其文本必定異於我們現存的手稿。該作者可能追循沙卡拉分派的某一細分，例如西希若分派（Saisira-sakha），我們知道沙烏那卡曾追循過此派。《大提婆書》所採用的《本集》（Sanhita）分法為曼陀羅、阿努瓦卡與讚歌（Mandalas、Anuvakas、Suktas）；但另一種分為八書（Ashtakas）的分法同樣著稱，甚至《補遺讚歌》（Khilas）也被計入，而沙烏那卡與卡提亞亞那二人皆排除了這些較晚出的讚歌——1 另一部《目錄》包含各曼陀羅的末尾詩句，沙德古魯希亞在其《目錄論》（Anukr. Bh.）第八卷第1處引述過。
參照《伊瓦‧曼陀經》第七卷第6至15段；《阿什塔卡經》第五卷第7至9段。

ANUKRAMANis。219 其索引性質迥然不同。庫恩博士據沙德古如西希亞（Shadgurusishya）《註釋》中的一段論文得出結論，認為《廣大提婆》（Brihaddevata）的作者不是索納卡（Saunaka），而是阿斯瓦拉亞納（Asvalayana）。然而，此一結論缺乏充分證據支持，且該著作本身引述索納卡之名，亦不足以成為駁斥索納卡著作權的有力論點。按照庫恩博士採用的論證路線，將《廣大提婆》歸於阿斯瓦拉亞納同樣會引起異議；蓋根據庫恩博士自身的校勘，該著作中有一處也出現了阿斯瓦拉亞納之名。此部奇特著作中引述的其他權威包括《愛達若耶迦》（Aitareyaka）、《考希塔金派》（Kaushitakins）、《跋羅婆梵書》（Bhallavi-brahmana）、《尼達那》（Nidana，尼達那聞名書籍 nidanasanjnake granthe）、薩卡羅派（Sakalas）、巴什卡羅派（Bashkalas）、馬都卡（Madhuka）、施維塔凱圖（Svetaketu）、迦羅婆（Galava）、迦爾吉亞（Gargya）、羅提塔羅（Rathitara）、羅坦達林（Rathantarin）、薩卡塔亞納（Sakatayana）、三迪勒耶（Sandilya）、羅摩迦亞納斯塔維羅（Romakayana Sthavira）、卡塔克亞（Kathakya）、跋古林（Bhagurin）、薩卡普尼（Sakapuni）、跋摩耶斯瓦（Bharmyasva）、牧德迦羅（Mudgala）、奧爾那瓦跋（Aurnavabha）、克勞什圖金（Kraushtukin）、馬特林（Matrin）及耶斯卡（Yaska）。最後者被引述最為頻繁，整部著作獻辭予他。由《廣大提婆》的文風判斷，雖然本著作原出自索納卡之手，但我們現今所持有的版本似乎經後世作者重新編訂。
以下數據取自沙烏那卡（Saunaka）的《經文索引》（Anukramanis），可以說明當時吠陀被研究的細密程度。根據沙烏那卡，梨俱吠陀的沙卡拉派本（Sakala-sakha）包含 10 部《曼荼羅》（Mandalas），或 64 部《阿笈多耶》（Adhyayas）。
曼荼羅。
阿紐瓦卡斯（Anuvakas）。

詩節。
我們必須留意根據《韋迪經》經分部（Varga）推導出的詩句數目，與紹那卡（Saunaka）所述數目之間的差異。後者給出的是 1 《三百六十四個阿地亞耶經》（Adhyayas）= 2,006 = 10,417 此處梵文原文為：Triṁ satani shatkanam chatvarinsat shat cha vargah.
《經索引》(ANUKRAMANI)第221頁：全部詩句總數=10,580，但隨即之後，半詩句的總數=21,232=10,616詩句。
這個差異是如何產生的，難以確定；然而應當注意的是，若將半詩句的總數二萬一千二百三十二除以二，我們得到一萬零六百一十六詩句，這個數字非常接近於一萬零六百二十二，這正是《遮羅那毘優訶》（Charanavyuha）所給出的梨俱吠陀詩句總數。根據《遮羅那毘優訶》（Charanavyuha, MS. Ch. 785.），梨俱吠陀的六十四卷（Adhyayas）具有：——
由梨俱吠陀詩節（rses Vargas）組成的經卷（Yargas）。
詩句　梨俱吠陀本集（Rig-vedasanhita）中pada或詞彙的數量據稱為153,826個，平均每首詩句約含14至15個詞彙。另一種計算方式將charcha-pada（即在Kramapatha中使用的詞彙，省略重複的段落或galitas）的數量定為110,704個，音節總數則為432,000個。
在另一份《經索》(Anukramani)中，沙烏納卡(Saunaka)提供了一份詩句清單，按照它們所採用的韻律編排；在末尾，他列舉了其總數。
